spec_match.rationale: The demo demonstrates the SVG displacement map filter applied to same-origin and blocked on cross-origin iframes correctly.

interactive.rationale: The visual presentation side-by-side with dynamic UA-sniffed status badge effectively shows the security fix.

blurb_alignment.rationale: The concept aligns directly with the SVG clickjacking vulnerability explanation in the feature index.

edge_cases.rationale: It demonstrates the main same-origin vs cross-origin case but does not allow interactive sandbox or other origin combinations.

references.rationale: The page successfully cites the chromestatus.com feature link and the original clickjacking writeup.

design_system.rationale: It adheres to the design system, using shared CSS variables and clear AA contrast states.

openQuestions.title: Support testing other iframe configurations interactively
openQuestions.detail: Add controls to toggle sandbox attributes (like allow-scripts, allow-same-origin) on the cross-origin iframe to see how different sandbox flags impact filter blocks dynamically.

openQuestions.title: Use capability detection over UA sniffing
openQuestions.detail: Currently, the status badge relies on Chrome version user-agent sniffing. Although detecting applied SVG filters programmatically on cross-origin iframes is difficult due to side-channel protection, explore whether a lightweight detection is possible or state the limitation clearly in code comments.

summary: The clickjacking replay demo is an outstanding visual representation of the security vulnerability and how Chrome 150 stops it, comparing a distorted same-origin iframe with an unaffected cross-origin iframe.
